coredata.dc:title: Photoactive coating based on waterborne polyurethane and carbon quantum dots as a prevention strategy for bacterial resistance
coredata.dc:creator: Dall Agnol, Lucas
Dias, Fernanda Trindade Gonzalez
Bianchi, Otávio
coredata.dc:description: This study aims to develop photoactive coatings based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and waterborne polyurethane (WPU), capable of preventing the formation of bacterial biofilms. CQDs synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis showed spherical morphology with an average size of 10.6 ± 3.1 nm, containing chemical groups with oxygen and nitrogen on the peripheral surface and photoluminescence at 460 nm and quantum yield of 63.2 % under excitation at 360 nm. Subsequently, CQDs were incorporated during WPU polymerization to form nanocomposites. The WPU/CQDs nanocomposites showed superior mechanical, rheological, and thermal properties concerning the pure WPU, due to the interaction of the nanoparticles with the polymeric matrix. The nanocomposites exhibited transparency and high photoluminescence, confirming the efficiency of the polymeric matrix in protecting the nanoparticles against photoluminescence extinction in the solid state. In addition, the WPU nanocomposite generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) after irradiation with blue light. The materials did not show cytotoxicity for BALB/3T3 murine fibroblasts after 24 h incubation. After illumination, the nanocomposite showed photodynamic activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram-negative), thus validating its potential as a photoactive coating with sterilizing properties.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to develop photoactive coatings based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and waterborne polyurethane (WPU), capable of preventing the formation of bacterial biofilms. CQDs synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis showed spherical morphology with an average size of 10.6 ± 3.1 nm, containing chemical groups with oxygen and nitrogen on the peripheral surface and photoluminescence at 460 nm and quantum yield of 63.2 % under excitation at 360 nm. Subsequently, CQDs were incorporated during WPU polymerization to form nanocomposites. The WPU/CQDs nanocomposites showed superior mechanical, rheological, and thermal properties concerning the pure WPU, due to the interaction of the nanoparticles with the polymeric matrix. The nanocomposites exhibited transparency and high photoluminescence, confirming the efficiency of the polymeric matrix in protecting the nanoparticles against photoluminescence extinction in the solid state. In addition, the WPU nanocomposite generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) after irradiation with blue light. The materials did not show cytotoxicity for BALB/3T3 murine fibroblasts after 24 h incubation. After illumination, the nanocomposite showed photodynamic activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram-negative), thus validating its potential as a photoactive coating with sterilizing properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Increased bacterial infections are becoming a significant problem worldwide, especially in hospitals and other healthcare facilities [1]. These infections account for 15 % of complications acquired by patients in the hospital during hospitalization or clinical procedures and are usually associated with multi-antibiotic-resistant microorganisms that prolong the patient's hospital stay, causing financial impacts on the health system [2]. The most common nosocomial infectious diseases are those of the urinary tract - caused by the use of probes, blood infections - favored by the use of catheters, pneumonia, and skin infections because of injections, venous access, surgical scar, or biopsy [2,3]. Medical devices attached to the patient (prostheses and catheters) represent about 60–70 % of hospital infections [3]. The surface of these devices is usually vulnerable to colonization by polymicrobial organisms (bacteria, fungi, and viruses). Since the surfaces of these devices can act as reservoirs of pathogens, their asepsis and sterilization are crucial to prevent contamination and indirect transmission [1,4].

Several approaches have been adopted to reduce microbial contamination, including the development of materials or surfaces impregnated with metallic nanoparticles known as germicidal (silver, copper, or molybdenum) or conventional antibiotics [1,5–7]. However, it has been scientifically proven that using these pathogen-eradication strategies has increased microbial resistance [3,5]. In addition, antimicrobial metallic materials can leach and diffuse active ions, which can cause local toxicity and increase the concentration in in non-target organs [8]. Polymer coatings based on polyethylene glycol (PEG), poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO), and polyurethanes (PU) have also been tested for inhibition of bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation [4,6]. Unfortunately, the effectiveness of these coatings is generally not comprehensive across all bacterial species. Thus, developing new strategies and more effective agents to fight infections becomes urgent and indispensable. An alternative to improve coatings' properties is combining polymers with photosensitizing nanomaterials (PS) to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). PS nanomaterials can eliminate bacteria by photothermal lysis or photoinduced acidification, representing a promising approach in antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (ADT) [8,9]. ROS are produced by the interaction of the PS agent with ultraviolet or visible light in the presence of air or any environment enriched with oxygen [1,6,8]. Unlike other antimicrobial agents, ROS does not have specific targets in the microorganism, decreasing the development of resistance. Furthermore, these radicals do little damage to mammalian cells, which are significantly more resistant to ROS than bacteria, offering a potential window for treatment [10]. Composites based on PU and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) modified with different PS molecules and nanoparticles (porphyrin, methylene blue, crystal violet/zinc oxide (ZnO), gold (Au)/methylene blue, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Ag) have been reported to be able to eradicate bacteria through the generation of ROS [1,9,11–13].

Besides antimicrobial therapies, CQDs can be used in sensing and imaging, optoelectronics, photocatalysis, and drug delivery. Substantial advances related to CQDs' synthesizing and physicochemical properties have already been reported. Various simple, low-cost, scalable synthesis routes of CQDs-related materials have been devised [14,15]. However, it remains challenging to correlate CQDs photoluminescence with their optical and electronic effects, which depends on understanding their surface-to-volume structure (chemical impurity, the abundance of functional groups, crystal defects, atomic vacations, etc.). When combined with a polymer matrix, the photoactivity of CQDs will also rely on their interaction with macromolecule chains, which needs to be better understood [7,9,16–22]. Besides, most of the CQDs used in nanocomposites display strong absorption in the UV region but fabricating CQDs through heteroatom doping or surface modification with visible- or NIR-harvesting ability is another demanded issue. Focusing on the antimicrobial application of CQDs-based materials, two requirements still need to be better investigated: (1) the mechanistic details of the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by different types of CQDs and their corresponding antimicrobial activities, and (2) comprehension of their cytotoxicity at specific concentrations, both in vitro and in vivo, especially on humans [23].

Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) can be an interesting polymer support to protect the photoluminescence (PL) of these nanoparticles due to its excellent water solubility. WPU ion centers can interact with CQDs in aqueous media to prevent solid-state quenching and result in an enhanced radiative transition, exhibiting increased photoluminescence properties [18]. The WPU coatings are permeable to gas, allowing an adequate amount of oxygen to be available to the PS [9,12,16,24]. This permeability can be tunable by combining suitable polyols and chain extenders, influencing the material's hydrogen bonding density and microphase separation [16]. Coatings based on WPU can be applied to different surfaces, such as wood, metal, glass, and other plastics, creating a protective barrier between the exterior and the coated surface. Despite the recognized advantages of using WPU and CQDs, studies involving the synthesis of WPU/CQD nanocomposites as antimicrobial coatings have not yet been performed, and few studies investigated the interaction of these nanoparticles with the PU matrix [7,9,16–22]. Therefore, this approach can bring new features to this kind of materials.

In this study, we intended to develop advanced materials based on WPU/CQDs, capable of self-disinfecting by the action of light and offering long-term prevention against biofilm formation or any microbial adhesion. The production of WPU/CQDs nanocomposites had a green technology character since WPU was synthesized without organic solvents [25]. The CQDs were obtained by microwave irradiation, a method that provides a high yield in a short reaction time [26]. The CQDs structure, morphology, and physicochemical properties were investigated. Special attention was paid to the ROS generation capacity, photolytic and antibacterial activities, cytotoxic and mechanical-structural properties of the WPU/CQDs nanocomposites. Thus, the materials presented here are promising in generating ROS and fighting microorganisms. Still, due to the low toxicity of CQDs this approach has good feasibility in applying smart coatings.

The following materials were supplied by Sigma Aldrich (USA): Hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI, ≥99 % purity), 2,2-bis(hydroxymethyl)propionic acid (DMPA, 98 %), triethylamine (TEA, ≥99.5 %), quinine sulfate, citric acid (CA, 98.5 %), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥99.5 %), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The aliphatic polycarbonate diol (PCD, Mw = 2000 g/mol and a hydroxyl value 56.3 mg KOH/g) based on a mixture of 1,5-pentanediol and 1,6-hexanediol (Eternacoll PH200) was supplied by UBE Corporation Europe (Spain). Before use, DMPA and PCD reagents were dried by vacuum under a pressure of 1330 Pa at 90 °C for 4 h. All solvents were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) was used in all experiments.

According to previous studies, CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of CA and EDA [27]. Briefly, CA and EDA at a 1:1 molar ratio were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasonication for 2 min (20 MHz). Then, the solution was irradiated for 6 min in a domestic microwave oven (650 W). Finally, the resulting red-brown solid was dissolved and dialyzed for three days with ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000). After, the CQDs were vacuum-dried at 65 °C (reaction yield was about 78 %).

The WPU/CQDs dispersion was prepared into a dry four-necked flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer, oil bath, condenser, dropping funnel, and nitrogen inlet. HDI and PCD reacted at 80 °C for 4 h with constant mechanical stirring (180 rpm) under a nitrogen atmosphere - to prepare the –NCO-terminated prepolymer. Next, DMPA was added to the reaction medium and reacted at 80 °C for 2 h. Acetone was added to control the viscosity of the reaction medium. Subsequently, the reaction temperature was reduced to 45 °C, and TEA was added (∼45 min) to neutralize the –COOH groups in the prepolymer. Finally, the prepolymer was dispersed in aqueous solutions containing the CQDs (0.0–2.0 wt%) under vigorous stirring (1100 rpm) for 30 min. The stoichiometric ratio of HDI/PCD/DMPA/TEA was 3:1:1:1.2. The residual solvents were removed by distillation under reduced pressure (60 °C for 1 h). The solids content at the dispersion was about 30 % by weight. Four nanocomposites named WPU0, WPU0.5, WPU1, and WPU2 were prepared using 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 wt% of CQDs, respectively. The films were prepared by evaporation of the WPU dispersion on a Teflon substrate and dried initially at 50 °C for 48 h and then at room temperature for 5 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structural analysis and photoluminescent properties
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded in a Perkin Elmer Impact 400 spectrometer (4000–400 cm−1, 64 scans, and 4 cm−1 resolution) using attenuated total reflectance mode (ATR, diamond at 45°). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigation was performed on an Omicron EA 125 model X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, with Al Kα radiation operating at 1486.6 eV.

Transmission electron (TEM) and atomic force (AFM) microscopies evaluated the CQDs' morphology. TEM images were performed on a JEOL JEM-1011 transmission electron microscope with 100 kV accelerating voltage. AFM images were performed on a Dimension Icon (Bruker) equipped with a ScanAsyst-air probe (Bruker).

The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained in a spectrophotometer (Evolution 60S Thermo Scientific) from 200 to 600 nm. The photoluminescence emission spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer LS 45 fluorescence spectrometer using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm from the 350–600 nm range. For all experiments, a 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette was used. For solid-state fluorescence and UV–Vis measurements of nanocomposite (ca. 0.6 mm thickness), the sample holder was positioned at a 30° angle from the incident beam.

The CQDs quantum yield (QY) was estimated by comparing their integrated photoluminescence intensities (excited at 360 nm) and absorbance values (at 360 nm) with that of a quinine sulfate standard in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54 %), according to the methodology described previously [27]. The size distribution of WPU/CQDs nanoparticles was determined at 25 °C by dynamic light scattering technique (DLS; Delsa™ Nano Series, Beckman Coulter) equipped with a laser diode (30 mW, λ = 658 nm) and light source at a 165° dispersion angle. The nanocomposite's molecular weight was determined by size exclusion chromatography (SEC) (Malvern HT-GPC chromatograph, refractive index detector), using tetrahydrofuran stabilized system (1 % BHT), 5 mg/mL sample concentration, 1 mL/min flow rate, 20 μL injected volume, and 45 °C column temperature. Polystyrene standards were used for the calibration curve.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) assessed the nanocomposites' thermal behavior. The DSC analyses were carried out in a DSC Q2000 equipment (TA Instruments) under a nitrogen atmosphere (50 mL/min) and a 10 °C/min heating rate. The samples (10–15 mg) were sealed in aluminum crucibles, heated from −80 °C to 180 °C, and kept at this temperature for 3 min to eliminate their thermal history. Then, the samples were cooled to −80 °C, kept at this temperature for 3 min, and reheated to 180 °C. Thermograms were obtained in a TGA-50 Shimadzu instrument, at a 25–800 °C range, under four heating rates (5, 10, 20, and 40 °C/min) and N2 atmosphere (50 mL/min). The methodology for assessing the thermal degradation kinetics (Friedman method - FR) and the thermal endurance of materials are described in Supplementary material.

The solid viscoelastic behavior of WPU/CQDs films was measured using a dynamic mechanical analyzer (TA Instruments, DMA 850 model), equipped with film clamp geometry, at −60 °C − 80 °C (5.0 μm oscillation amplitude, 1 Hz frequency, 3 °C/min heating rate, and nitrogen atmosphere). Samples with 15.0 mm × 6.0 mm × 1.0 mm dimensions were used. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

Small-amplitude oscillatory shear measurements were performed in an Anton Parr MCR 101 rheometer using plate-plate geometry (25 mm diameter, 0.5 mm gap). The analyses were conducted at 120 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere using frequency sweep mode (0.1–100 rad/s) and strain amplitude of 1 % (linear viscoelastic regime).

Mechanical properties (tensile strength and elongation at break) were evaluated on an Emic DL2000 Universal Testing Machine equipped with a 500 N load cell operated at a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min (ASTM D638). Samples with dimensions 10 cm × 1 cm × 0.3 cm were used. Hardness tests were performed according to ASTM D2240 using a Bareiss Shore A analog hardness tester on a 6 mm thick plate with 3 s of measurement time. All mechanical tests were conducted at 23 °C, and at least five replicates for each specimen were made to report the average.

The surface free energy of the WPU and WPU/CQDs films was evaluated by the Owens–Wendt method [28]. The contact angle was determined using a SEO® Phoenix100 (Korea) instrument with four different liquids at 23 ± 2 °C with distinct superficial tension (γL), dispersive (non-polar) (γL D), and polar (γL P) components [29]: distilled water, glycerin, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and hexadecane. In order to avoid errors caused by gravity acting on the sessile drop, the droplet volume was set to 5 μL in all experiments. The contact angle was measured at least ten times at different sites on the films' surface to consider the average value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Leaching behavior of CQDs in aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Visible spectroscopy technique investigated the leaching behavior of CQDs from the nanocomposite in an aqueous medium. An amount of 0.2 g of the film was immersed in 3 mL of ultrapure water for 1700 h. The UV–Vis absorbance of the aqueous solution containing the film was periodically measured to monitor any CQDs leaching from the polymer into the surroundings. All experiments were done in triplicate. The calibration curve for quantifying the CQDs leached content was constructed within the 10 mg/L–100 mg/L concentration range, using ultrapure water as solvent.

For the swelling water behavior, three samples of each film (30 × 10 × 0.3 mm) were initially dried at 60 °C for 48 h in a circulating air oven, thus obtaining the dry weight of the samples (W
d ). The samples were then soaked in water, and their mass gain was monitored, allowing the determination of the wet weight of the materials (W
w ). Finally, the water uptake (WU) was calculated by Eq. (1) [29]:
(1)
WU =
W w
−
W d
W d
· 100 %
Measurements were done three times for each sample, and the average value was recorded as mean and standard deviation (±SD).

The ability of the WPU/CQDs nanocomposite to generate 1O2 upon visible light illumination was determined by the oxidative bleaching of the known 1O2 quencher DPBF due to its fast reaction with 1O2 [11]. Briefly, a piece of nanocomposite film (2 × 2 cm) was placed in contact with 3 mL of a methanol solution containing 18.5 μM of DPBF and then illuminated by a portable laser (532 nm, 100 ± 1 mW/cm2; WP Laser 303) at 23 °C for 90 min. The DPBF absorption decay percentage, which is proportional to the 1O2 production, could be assessed by the difference between the initial absorbance and the absorbance after a given period of irradiation. Each experiment was repeated three times. For comparison, the control experiments were performed without the photosensitizer, confirming that 1O2 generation is derived from CQDs and not from solvent and DPBF effects.

Neutral Red Uptake (NRU) assay was used to analyze the potential toxicity of WPU/CQDs nanocomposite on murine fibroblast cells BALB/3T3 clone A31 (ATCC CCL163), according to ISO 10993-5 (2009) [32]. BALB/3T3 cells (CAS 001242, Rio de Janeiro Cell Bank, Brazil) were cultured (37 °C, 5 % CO2 and 95 % humidity) in DMEM with 4 mM l-glutamine, adjusted to 1.5 g/L sodium bicarbonate and 4.5 g/L glucose, and bovine calf serum to a final concentration of 10 %. When cells reached 80 % confluence, they were detached using 0.2 % (w/v) trypsin and transferred to new culture flasks. Before the experiments, the nanocomposite films (6 cm2) were sterilized by ultraviolet light for 30 min. Sterilized samples were incubated in 3 cm2/mL of supplemented DMEM for 24 h. The release medium was, after incubation, diluted with fresh medium at different concentrations (pure extract and the following four concentrations were diluted 10 % from the previous concentration) and tested in contact with cells for 24 h. Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 104 cells/well for 24 h before assay. The culture medium in each well was then replaced with extracts; the positive control or negative control was inserted into 96-cluster-well-culture plates. The positive control is sodium lauryl sulfate, as indicated in the OECD protocol 129. After incubation for 24 h, cells were washed with PBS, and the cell viability was evaluated. Neutral red dye dissolved in the serum of free medium (100 μg/mL) was added to the culture medium and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Cells were washed with PBS, and 150 μL of elution medium (50 % ethanol and 1 % glacial acetic acid in water) was added followed by gentle shaking for 60 min for complete dissolution. The absorbance of each well was recorded at 540 nm wavelength using a microtiter plate reader. Non-treated cell (in DMEM) was used as control and the relative cell viability (mean% ± SD, n = 3) was expressed as Abssample/Abscontrol × 100 %, where Abssample and Abscontrol are the absorbances of the well containing the nanocomposite and the control (without nanocomposite), respectively. Based on Abssample and Abscontrol values, cytotoxicity degree can then be categorized as: severe cytotoxic (<30 %), moderate cytotoxic (30–60 %), slight cytotoxic (60–90 %), and not cytotoxic (>90 %) [33].

The photocatalytic antibacterial activity of the neat WPU and WPU/CQDs materials was investigated according to ISO 22196, using the bacterial strains Staphylococcus aureus (MTCC 3160, Gram-positive) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MTCC 1688, Gram-negative). These bacterial species were purchased from ATCC (American Type Culture Collection) and were selected because they are common in hospital environments [10]. WPU1 and WPU0 (control) were UV sterilized (258 nm lamp) for 4 h before the microbiological tests. The samples (2.5 cm × 2.5 cm) were placed in a 24-well plate and covered with 0.1 mL PBS containing 1 × 104 CFU/mL of bacteria. The 24-well plate was kept in an incubator at 37 °C under light exposure (100 W, 50 cm sample distance, λ ≥ 360 nm, 24 h), while another 24-well plate was reserved in the dark (as a dark control) for the same time period. Upon illumination, the material was removed from the 24-well plate, and the absorbance was read at 600 nm (microplate reader SpectraMax M2e). Then, 0.01 % resazurin was added to the wells, and colorimetric differences were observed. The results were compared concerning the positive control. The negative control is based on the culture medium without the inoculum. All microbiological assays were performed in triplicate, and data were presented as mean ± standard error of means (n = 3). One-way ANOVA was used to compare the means of different data sets, and P < 0.05 indicated statistical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structural analysis and photoluminescent properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR and XPS techniques investigated the chemical composition and major functional groups on the synthesized CQDs' surface. As shown in the FTIR spectrum [Fig. 1(a)], the EDA exhibited characteristic absorption bands of −NH2 (3352–3276 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1), CH2 (2920 and 2852 cm−1) [34]. CA mainly has oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH at 3371 and 3231 cm−1), C−H (3036 cm−1), CO (1728 cm−1) and C−O (1213 and 1112 cm−1) which indicated the existence of –COOH [35]. The CQDs mainly have carbon, oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, in the FTIR spectra include stretching of −OH/−NH (3406 and 3065 cm−1), stretching of CO (1651 cm−1), deformation of −NH/C−N (1558 cm−1 and 1228 cm−1) and asymmetric deformation of C–O–C (1062 cm−1 e 955 cm−1), as well as asymmetric stretch and bending vibration of −C–H (2964 and 1369 cm−1), which indicated the formation of amide and carboxylic acid linkages during reaction [27,35–37]. Meanwhile, in the XPS full scan spectrum [Fig. 1(b)], three peaks at 286.1 (C 1s), 401.2 (N 1s), and 533.3 eV (O 1s) were detected, indicating that CQDs consist essentially of carbon (39.20 %), nitrogen (17.18 %), and oxygen elements (43.61 %) [27]. The C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks, corresponding to C−C/C=C (285.1 eV), sp3 carbon (C−O/C−N, 286.6 eV), carbonyl carbons (CO, 287.6 eV), and carboxylic carbons (–COOH, 289.0 eV [Fig. 1(c)] [35]. The N 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.2, 401.1, and 401.9 eV, representing pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and N−H, respectively [Fig. 1(d)] [35]. The O 1s spectrum contains two peaks at 532.9 eV (C=O) and 534.9 eV (C−O−C/C−OH) [Fig. 1(e)] [27]. The results of XPS and FTIR corroborate in the composition and types of functional groups. As CQDs have polar groups on the surface, they are soluble in polar solvents such as water, which favors their homogeneous distribution and dispersion in the WPU matrix. The size of the synthesized CQDs was determined by AFM and TEM techniques. CQDs were nearly spherical with an average size of 10.6 ± 3.1 nm [Fig. 1(f), (g), and (f) inset].

Regarding photoluminescent behavior, CQDs exhibited two UV–Vis absorption bands, one at 250 nm and the other at 360 nm (Fig. S1). The band at 250 nm was attributed to the π–π* electronic transition of aromatic CC bonds originating from the nucleus. The band at 360 nm was related to the n − π* transition of CO and C−N bonds, probably present on the CQDs surface, as identified in FTIR and XPS analysis [27,35]. Furthermore, the CQDs showed maximum fluorescent emission at 460 nm under excitation at 360 nm. CQDs solutions exhibited light yellow and intense fluorescent blue colors when observed under visible and UV light (at excitation of 360 nm), respectively [see the inset in Fig. S1(a)]. The CQDs optical properties were further evaluated from photoluminescence spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths (320–440 nm). They exhibited a maximum emission at 460 nm, and maximal fluorescence intensity was achieved at 360 nm excitation wavelength [Fig. S1(b)]. Furthermore, the maximum emission did not vary as a function of the excitation wavelength, confirming the results of TEM and AFM regarding the uniform size and surface properties of the CQDs [35,37]. Interestingly, the nanoparticles showed a QY of 63.2 % at 360 nm excitation, a result far superior to those previously reported [13,35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size distribution of WPU nanoparticles and chemical structure
ce:section.ce:para:
The particle size distribution, average hydrodynamic diameter, molecular weight (M
n , M
w ), and polydispersion index (M
w /M
n ) of the WPU/CQDs composites are shown in Table 1 . WPU particles show a size trend with increasing CQDs content, probably due to chemical interactions of this polyfunctional compound. Large particles (>1 μm) generally result in unstable dispersions due to precipitation phenomena [17]. In this work, samples of up to 1.0 wt% of CQDs were more stable, with no evidence of apparent sedimentation for more than six months. Dispersions formulated with CQDs contents higher than 1.0 wt% were unstable for long periods. When 2.0 wt% of CQDs were added to the WPU, the amount of water in the phase inversion reaction step had to be doubled, even so, precipitation occurred after one week. Because CQDs are multifunctional, these nanoparticles end up restricting the segmental movement of the polymeric chain during phase inversion when high concentrations are used. As a result, there is no suitable organization of the chains in a core-shell structure, in which the hydrophobic flexible segments are surrounded by hydrophilic rigid segments, thus contributing to a broader particle size distribution. However, small amounts of CQDs (<2.0 wt%) can be interesting in obtaining specific properties to tune the relative hydrogen bond density and phase separation in the WPU-based system [18,19].

FTIR analysis also confirmed the nanomaterials' chemical composition and the structural changes resulting from the CQDs and WPU matrix interaction. The characteristic absorption bands of the WPU were present in all spectra of Fig. S3: 3360–3420 cm−1 (NH deformation), 2955 cm−1 and 2865 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching of CH2), 1738–1637 cm−1 (C=O), 1525 cm−1 (CN and NH deformation), 1238 cm−1 and 955 cm−1 (C–O–C asymmetric deformation) [17,20,29]. However, no significant differences were observed between the spectra of the neat WPU and the nanocomposites, suggesting that the polymer absorptions overlap those of the CQDs-WPU reaction. The only distinction between these spectra is the absorption bands at 1738 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 related to the CO of the urethane group [21]. This region gives information about the phase separation of the PU [40]. Thus, these bands were deconvoluted in PeakFit® software (Fig. S4) to reveal more information regarding CQDs-WPU interactions. The following equations were used to analyze the deconvoluted bands: [X
o = A1690 / (A1690 + A1711 + A1740)], [X
d = A1711 / (A1690 + A1711 + A1740)], and [X
b = (X
o + X
d )]; where X
b , X
o , and X
d represent the degree of hydrogen bonding and the percentages of ordered and disordered hydrogen bonds, respectively. Variations in band areas were detected for the incorporation of CQDs into the nanocomposites (Table S2), demonstrating the influence of nanoparticles on the relative amount of hydrogen bonds. The area of the band related to hydrogen-bonded carbonyl becomes more significant, while the area related to free carbonyl decreases. In addition, X
b values also increased as the CQDs content increased. As mentioned, the polar functional groups of CQDs quickly form hydrogen bonds, which may contribute to the greater phase separation between the rigid and flexible segments in the matrix [16,20,21].

The behavior observed for T
gSS and T
gHS suggests that incorporating CQDs increase phase separation between the rigid and flexible segments of the nanocomposites. To confirm this assumption, the phase separation degree was calculated by the difference between the nanomaterial's T
gSS and the glass transition temperature of the pure flexible segments (T
gSS0) (for PCD = −52.26 °C, evaluated by DSC) [44]. The higher the difference (T
gSS
–T
gSS0 ), the greater the miscibility or compatibility between rigid and flexible segments. The T
gSS of WPU may be related to the fraction of the rigid segment dispersed in the flexible phase (w
H ). By the general principle of addition, which states that a change in the thermal property of a two-component single-phase system is the linear addition of two changes in this property in the individual components, the following relationship can be considered [44]:
(2)
T gSS
=
1 −
w H
T
gHS 0
+
w H
T
g HS
0
where T
gSS is the T
g of the flexible phase, and T
gHS0 is the glass transition of the rigid segment formed by the reaction between HDI and DMPA (140.86 °C, evaluated by DSC). The “T
gSS
–T
gSS0 ” increases for the nanocomposite systems (Table 2 ) confirming the interaction between the CQDs' surface groups and WPU, and thus, the enhancement of rigid-flexible segments miscibility [43]. WPUs inherently show a high degree of hard-soft phase separation, as verified by the low values of “T
gSS
–T
gSS0 ”. The same is observed when comparing the values of w
H . For example, the neat WPU (WPU0) presented a w
H = 0.08, the same value for the nanocomposites, not even with the incorporation of 2.0 wt% CQDs, suggesting that the amount of rigid phase dispersed in the flexible phase does not change.

Table 2 shows that the variation of heat capacity (ΔC
p ) of T
gSS increased with CQDs content, probably due to the changes in the flexible segment involved in the transition. The phase separation degree was also calculated considering the estimation of Camberlin and Pascault [45] (Table 2). As expected, the phase separation degree in the nanocomposites increased as a function of CQDs content, which is probably related to the molecular mobility restriction at the segments interface.

Fig. 3(b) shows the dependence of activation energies (E
α ) on the degree of conversion (α (T)) calculated by the Friedman method (FR), using four different heating rates. From the profile of the E
α vs. α (T), it is possible to confirm that the thermal degradation is composed of three steps, the first at 0 ≤ α ≤ 0.2, the second at 0.2 ≤ α ≤ 0.6, and the third at 0.6 ≤ α ≤ 1. Furthermore, were obtained the mean values for E
α and A (pre-exponential factor) for WPU0 as E
α = 124.8 kJ/mol and A = 8.48 s−1 and for WPU1 E
α = 133.6 kJ/mol and A = 9.01 s−1. These values agree with the literature data and suggest an increase in the thermal stability of the first and second degradation steps of WPU1 compared to WPU0 [46].

As demonstrated in previous results (FTIR and XPS), CQDs contain carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amino groups on their surface capable of interacting strongly with the flexible and rigid segments of WPU, affecting its original phase behavior. According to the literature [44,47], particulate polymeric systems may have the flexibility determined by the increase in the viscous component caused by the excess of particles or by the slippage phenomenon that occurs in the presence of small particles. Although CQDs can act as restriction points of segmental movement due to their interactions and increased phase separation, systems with small spherical particles behave anomalously to classical composites [48,49].

All WPU/CQDs composites showed a pseudo-solid mechanical behavior (Fig. 4 ). The incorporation of nanoparticles increased the storage module (E′) at higher temperatures in the glassy region. Furthermore, the T
gSS values increased as a function of CQDs content: −32.3 °C (WPU0), −23.5 °C (WPU0.5), −21.9 °C (WPU1) and −26.7 °C (WPU2). Above 40 °C, T
gHS values for samples can be detected as 48.1 °C (WPU0), 52.3 °C (WPU0.5), 52.8 °C (WPU1) and 52.8 °C (WPU2). This increase in T
gHS , resulting from the presence of CQDs, is probably associated with hydrogen interactions between the CQDs and the CO groups of the WPU. Similar results have also been reported in the literature [43].

In the elastic plateau region, the E′ value of WPU0 was lower than those of the nanocomposites, except for WPU2. By the theory of elasticity, this behavior could be attributed to an increase in the system's molar mass [50]. However, SEC results (Table 1) did not identify differences in molar masses for the materials, not even when high concentrations of CQDs were employed. Therefore, the alteration in E′ values in the plateau region for samples containing CQDs may be associated with the interaction between the CQDs and the WPU matrix and the small particles slippage promoted in the system, instead of a molar mass influence [47–49].

Since the polydispersions of the molar masses are close, it is difficult to clearly state about the formation of branched chains. Besides, the dissipation factor in the solid state changes a bit in the glassy region with the increase of CQDs. However, in the elastic plateau region, the WPU0 sample showed higher values for tan δ, what reflects that both E′ and elastic modulus (E″) are decreasing with the addition of CQDs.

The effect of incorporating CQDs on the molten state rheological properties of WPU was investigated. Fig. 5 shows the dependence of complex viscosity (η*) as a function of angular frequency (ω) for neat WPU and nanocomposites. Complex viscosity, η* decreases with increasing content of CQDs, especially at low frequencies, which may be related to the lubrication caused by nanoparticles [48,51]. For polymers with similar molar masses, the incorporation of small particles (d < 10 nm) into a highly viscous liquid can cause a decrease in zero shear viscosity (η
0 ) values. This lubricating effect changes a shift in the liquid behavior of polymer systems, which is predicted by Einstein's Suspension Spheres Law and has been reported for poly(vinylidene fluoride)/silsesquioxane oligomeric polyhedra (PVDF/POSS) nanocomposites [48]. As polyhedral-shaped silsesquioxane structures are also endowed with superficial functional groups, any similarity in rheological behavior with CQDs-based nanocomposites is reasonable [48]. Besides, it should be considered that the critical concentration for agglomeration in polymer/spherical nanoparticle systems is 0.2 wt% [48,51]. The clusters can have pseudo-liquid behavior, which also contributes to the reduction of viscosity [51]. These particles increase the viscous component when agglomerated, decreasing the mechanical properties and Tg. This behavior was observed in systems based on polymer/small particles [49,51].

Table 3 presents the rheological parameters determined by fitting the Cross equation [38]. The WPU/CQDs composites have a pseudoplastic behavior, characterized by a decrease in viscosity as a function of the increasing oscillation frequency. The correlation coefficients for the Cross model fits were above 0.99. The increase in CQDs content caused the reduction of m and λ values. A high value of the time constant (λ) implies the need for a relatively high oscillatory shear to cause structural failure. When λ is large (the time-dependent parameter associated with the Newtonian plateau transition is large), the breakdown occurs at relatively low shear rates (frequencies). Therefore, the transition from the Newtonian plateau occurs at lower frequencies, which is attributed to the increase in viscous dissipation. The ƞ
0 * parameter decreases as the CQDs content increase, and although it indicates the molar mass, the SEC results show they are similar between all samples. Considering that CQDs are polyfunctional, it is believed that a bound moiety is incapable of interacting with more than two WPU chains for contents up to 2.0 wt% of nanoparticles. However, the crosslinked polymer can be formed for CQDs contents higher than 2.0 wt%. This is also reflected in the larger particle size observed in the suspensions, measured by DLS. The m values for the composites tended to be zero (m → 0 = Newtonian behavior), and their shear thinning behavior decreased with the increase in CQDs content. This behavior is directly related to the slipping effect that these small particles cause in the polymer chains.

The presence of small nanoparticles affects cooperative dynamics and viscoelastic properties, as the literature has shown for POSS systems with particle sizes <1.5 nm [48,51]. The anomalous reduction in viscosity is a recurrent feature. Wang and Hill [52] reported that the viscosity reduction is because of the formation of a polymer layer at the nanoparticle-polymer interface, which has a different viscosity and density than the pure polymer. The theoretical model described by these authors also showed that even small fractions of nanoparticles would induce viscosity reduction. Kalathi et al. [53] performed molecular dynamics simulations of mixtures of nanoparticles and weakly interacting polymers. They showed that nanoparticles smaller than the entanglement mesh size would induce faster rates of monomeric relaxation and chain organization. More recently, Senses et al. [54] demonstrated that small nanoparticles induce the dilation of the retraction tube and, consequently, the chain deinterlacing if the particle size is smaller than the entanglement mesh size. Thus, CQDs could induce a viscosity reduction process to modify the WPU chains' relaxation.

Table 4 shows the mechanical properties of the films, the incorporation of 1.0 wt% CQDs to WPU caused a considerable increase in tensile strength, elongation at break, and hardness of the nanocomposite. However, this effect was not as significant considering the addition of 0.5 wt% CQDs. The incorporation of CQDs increases the interfacial tension and topological secondary interactions with the matrix, causing an increase in the system's rigidity and, therefore, an increase in the tensile strength [16,17,20]. In these interactions, the contribution of hydrogen bonds at the diffuse interface formed between the CQDs' surface groups and the rigid and flexible segments must be considered [20]. This diffuse interfacial layer between the WPU segments plays a transitional role when the matrix is stretched, which causes an increase in elongation at break. However, this increase in mechanical properties did not occur for CQDs content higher than 1.0 wt%, which may be associated with a critical limit in the WPU phase separation [17]. As previously mentioned, high content of nanoparticles can favor the agglomeration and the slippage of polymer chains, which do not contribute to mechanical strength resistance [47,51]. Considering the application of these composites on the surfaces of the coatings, the WPU1 formulation was the most promising.

WPUs have been widely applied to various substrates such as leather, glass, wood, plastics, metals, concrete, and ceramics [55]. It is known that the surface free energy (ɣ
S ) of these substrates is the main parameter that influences the wettability and adhesion of the coating, and they will also affect bacterial adhesion [1]. The surface properties of the materials were investigated, and the results are reported in the Supplementary material (Table S4). The ɣ
S and ɣ
S
P parameters of the nanocomposites increased with higher contents of CQDs at room temperature (WPU0: ɣ
S = 30.9 mN/m and ɣ
S
P = 8.3 mN/m; WPU1: ɣ
S = 33.3 mN/m and ɣ
S
P = 10.7 mN/m), which agree with the literature [9,29,55]. The presence of CQDs in the polymeric matrix provided hydrophilicity to nanocomposites. High content of CQDs in the nanocomposite provides more –COOH and –OH polar groups affording hydrophilicity to the polymer. In general, the low ɣ
S of the WPU is associated with the localized polarity of the rigid segments and ionic groups on the material's surface [56]. Gallu et al. [41] synthesized thermoplastic polyurethanes (TPUs) with different rigid-to-flexible segment ratios. They reported that the materials became more polar as the ratio of the rigid segment increased (components of higher polarity and hydrogen bonding). Some studies suggest that bacterial adhesion is favored when the contact angle between the surface and water is close to 70°, while higher or lower contact angles decrease this susceptibility [22]. Discussion about hydrophobicity's effect on bacterial strains' colonization is still contradictory in the literature, but a general trend is that hydrophilic surfaces attract more bacteria [57]. The mechanism of bacterial adhesion and interaction with a substrate will not only be dictated by the value of ɣ
S . Still, it will also depend on the substrate roughness and microorganism nature - its structural and surface characteristics, such as the electrical charge in the microorganism membrane.

The solubility of WPU0 and WPU1 films was evaluated in thirty-five different solvents and classified as insoluble (value = 0) or soluble (value = 1). As shown in Fig. 6 , all solvents that solubilized the materials were inside the HSP sphere, while the insoluble ones remained outside. This behavior was also observed for the relative energy difference (RED) data reported in Table S1, where RED < 1 represents strong interaction between film and solvent and RED > 1 denotes weak interaction between them. The HSPs values obtained for WPU0 and WPU1 materials were similar [δ D (24.86 MPa0.5), δ P (8.21 MPa0.5), δ H (7.64 MPa0.5) and R
0 (16.31)] and close to those found in the literature for TPUs [41]. Gallu et al. [41] reported that increasing the content of rigid segments in a TPU led to higher solubility parameters and a decrease in the R0 value, due to elevated polar and hydrogen bond components. This suggested that TPU becomes more polar as the rigid segment fraction increases. A similar result was found for the surface energy of WPU/CQDs nanocomposites. However, Mieczkowski et al. [58] showed that the PUs solubility parameters are very dependent on the molecular architecture of the flexible segment, which could explain the HSPs similarity between WPU0 and WPU1. Incorporating CQDs in the WPU structure has little effect on the flexible phase, affecting its rigid phase and microphase separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Leaching of CQDs in an aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para: One of the main concerns about using nanoparticles in polymer materials is the possible leaching of these from the matrix, which can result in secondary pollution and a health risk to non-target organisms. The leaching rate of CQDs from WPU1 film was investigated in aqueous media employing UV–Visible spectroscopy technique (Fig. S5). In the first 24 h, only 0.2 wt% of the nanoparticles had been leached into the medium. This initial leaching of CQDs is probably attributed to the nanoparticles adsorbed on the film's surface, which migrated immediately to the solution [56]. In a 500 h interval of analysis, the leaching rate of these nanoparticles was slow, only 1.5 wt% were transferred to the medium. After this period, the leaching rate quickly increased to 7 wt% and then reached stability. It is noteworthy that, during the polymerization, not all CQDs were covalently bonded to the WPU, even so, these nanoparticles showed resistance to leaching from the matrix due to their interaction with WPU. Additionally, CQDs leaching may be caused by the swelling of the nanocomposite film [Fig. S5(b)]. These increase the spacing between polymer chains, causing non-WPU-bound CQDs to migrate to the medium [47]. Low water resistance has been another key factor in restricting the use of WPUs in advanced applications [17]. This water absorption behavior by the WPU-based film is influenced by many parameters, including the structure of the hydrophobic network, the material's crosslinking density, and the presence of polar nanoparticles in the polymer chains. After 1700 h, the swelling value for WPU0 was 105 %, while for WPU1 was 145 %. The presence of CQDs made the polar peripheral structure of WPU-based nanocomposite more hydrophilic and less resistant to water.

Fig. 7(a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the films. Compared to the spectrum of the neat WPU, the nanocomposite spectra showed broad absorption bands with maximums at 360 nm, characteristic of the CQDs. With increasing CQDs concentration, also increased the absorption peak intensity. The same effect was detected for the fluorescence properties of the material [Fig. 7(b)]. The solid-state films emitted fluorescence, with a maximum emission at 460 nm, when excited at 360 nm, successfully confirming the incorporation of nanoparticles to WPU without affecting their original specific properties [16,17]. Bai et al. [21] synthesized TPU/CQDs films that exhibited higher absolute QY values when compared to those measured for isolated CQDs. The authors attributed this enhanced emission effect to better CQDs preservation when incorporated into the crosslinked polymeric structure. The nanocomposite crosslinked structure remains efficiently immobilized during the rotation and vibration of CQDs functional groups, which enhances radiative transition and, thus, fluorescence properties.

Fig. 7 shows the optical characteristics of the nanocomposites when observed under visible and UV lights. While the WPU0 film is colorless and transparent, the nanocomposites showed increased levels of yellow coloration under visible light illumination. The colorimetry technique measured this color change caused by nanoparticles in the polymer (Table S5). The results showed that increasing the concentration of CQDs makes the nanocomposite films darker (ΔL*) and with a higher yellowing index (Δb*). In addition, all samples exhibited small values for green-red index difference (Δa). These colorimetric characteristics are intrinsic to solid-state CQDs themselves. However, the visible light transmittance and the original transparency of WPU were maintained, even with the incorporation of CQDs, because of the nanoparticles quantum size and uniform distribution throughout the polymer matrix [18]. In spite of that, this minor change in color did not significantly alter the material's characteristics when used in coatings.

The 1O2 production by the WPU1 nanocomposite was estimated considering the decrease of the spectroscopic absorbance intensity at 410 nm by the DPBF compound during laser irradiation onto the films (532 nm wavelength). Fig. 8(a) shows the absorbance spectra of the DPBF solution in methanol recorded during WPU1 illumination at different time intervals. The reaction between 1O2 species derived from CQDs-based nanocomposite and DPBF molecules generating 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene. Considering the short lifetime of these species (∼12 μs), the WPU1 nanocomposite can function as a 1O2 reservoir for a relatively long time after excitation (several tens of μs), being able to gradually release singlet oxygen to the environment, where it is effectively extinguished [1]. The experiments performed in pure methanol (control without the nanocomposite) or without lighting (dark control with WPU1), no significant absorption reduction of the DPBF was observed [Fig. 8(b)]. There is no auto-oxidation of DPBF by oxygen, which maintains its band at 410 nm unchanged [11]. These results provide evidence that 1O2 generation is due to the nanocomposite under visible light and have also been observed for other similar systems [11,59].

Low toxicity is one of the most critical requirements for any material with an application in medical or biomedical technology. In this context, the cell viability of WPU/CQDs nanocomposites upon BALB/3T3 was tested through the NRU assay. The materials WPU0 and WPU1 did not show cytotoxicity against BALB/3T3 cells after treatment for 24 h, regardless the extract concentration (Fig. 9 ). Cell viability results above 100 % could be explained as a direct compensation response to low levels of stress or damage when disruption of homeostasis is achieved, similar to cell control [32]. These results allow us to infer that such nanocomposites would not cause cytotoxic effects if applied as a medical device for short-term contact with human body. Other researches have already reported that the presence of CQDs in the WPU matrix cannot alter its biological response [7,9,22].

The WPU/CQDs nanocomposite antibacterial activity was investigated by in vitro photodynamic inactivation assays with and without light irradiation (360 nm), in the presence of S. aureus (Gram-positive) and P. aeruginosa (Gram-negative) bacterial strains. The results are shown in Fig. 10 . WPU1 demonstrated a 40 % reduction in P. aeruginosa activity after light exposure (λ ≥ 360 nm, 100 W; 24 h). No significant inactivation was observed for WPU0, with or without irradiation. The WPU1 nanocomposite also did not exhibit any inactivation when examined without light irradiation (dark control). However, WPU1 showed a significant antibacterial effect against P. aeruginosa specie when irradiated by UV light. Despite the differences in the cell wall structure of P. aeruginosa and S. aureus, the targets and cell death mechanisms were expected to be similar or identical for both strains [60]. However, this behavior was not observed. Nie et al. [11] reported this selectivity in the antibacterial photodynamic activity of CQDs synthesized from CA and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene by a solvothermal process. Jiang et al. [8] noted similar results for antimicrobial photosensitizers based on manganese-doped zinc sulfide quantum dots.

Comparing bacterial efficiency values absolutely with other studies is challenging because bacteria killing by photodynamic therapy depends on many factors: the type of used polymers and photosensitizers, the intensity of the applied light source, the lifetime of singlet oxygen and concentration of used photosensitizers, and irradiation time. A possible explanation for the inactivation of S. aureus is that it tends to form grape-like clusters, which limits the diffusibility of 1O2 cytotoxic species generated by CQDs [11,57,59]. The better efficacy of WPU1 against P. aeruginosa suggests that these CQDs have positively charged surface groups that interacted electrostatically with this negatively charged bacterial strain [61–64]. CQDs with higher nitrogen bonding content can produce reactive nitrogen species (RNO) synergistically with ROS [23]. Since CQDs can be synthesized from different precursors and synthetic strategies, their antibacterial photodynamic activity will significantly depend on their surface charge characteristics. So, for comparing antibacterial efficiency numbers with other publications, it would be necessary to consider CQDs of the exact nature. Another point to be assumed is that each film evaluated for its antibacterial activity was irradiated only from one of its sides, which is probably correlated with the observed % survival of P. aeruginosa. Despite this exploratory antibacterial activity assessment, WP1 nanocomposite needs to improve its biological efficiency.

This study presents the synthesis of photoactive coatings based on waterborne polyurethane and carbon quantum dots as a prevention strategy for bacterial resistance. The CQDs were prepared by a rapid microwave-assisted pyrolysis method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen precursors. The nanoparticles have 10.6 ± 3.1 nm as average size and photoluminescence at 460 nm when excited at 360 nm with a 63.2 % quantum yield. These CQDs were incorporated during the phase inversion step of WPU polymerization to form nanocomposites. Although the CQDs can act as restrictive points for segmental movement due to their interactions, the WPU/CQDs composites showed evidence that a portion of the CQDs are not linked by covalent bonds to the WPU. CQDs can also act on the mobility gain of the polymeric chains due to the sliding effect these small nanoparticles cause in the chains, changing the dynamic-mechanical behavior and reducing the viscosity of the materials. The generation of singlet oxygen species was confirmed using irradiation. In all evaluated extract concentrations of the films, the nanocomposites showed biocompatibility and non-cytotoxicity for BALB/3T3. Furthermore, the WPU1 nanocomposite exhibited antibacterial photodynamic activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram-negative), reducing its viability by about 40 % in 24 h irradiation at 360 nm. Overall, the results demonstrate that WPU/CQDs nanocomposites are low toxicity-promising materials for surface coatings with light-activated sterilizing properties with low toxicity.
